title: Coffee on Leather
timeEstimate: Pro-lane for any meaningful spot; home is blot + condition only
stainChemistry: Coffee = browning pigments + chlorogenic acid + tannin. Coffee with cream adds milk milk protein + butterfat. On finished leather, fresh coffee often blots off the topcoat before reaching the dyed hide. On aniline leather, coffee soaks straight into the dye. The CRITICAL leather rule applies: NO oxidizers — peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine strip the finish + dye + oils permanently. Pro uses sodium hydrosulfite (REDUCING bleach, leather-safe) for residual tannin. Cotton coffee-removal toolkit (peroxide) destroys leather.
whyThisWorks: Quick blot prevents coffee from soaking past the topcoat. Mild soap + minimum water + conditioning preserves the hide. Pro reducing-bleach chemistry handles residual tannin without stripping leather. Home options: blot fast, mild wipe, condition, escalate.
defaultAssumption: Finished leather assumed; aniline = pro from minute one. Coffee with cream adds protein-before-heat ordering — cold blot, then pro.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Drop of water, white cloth, cold water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Water-drop test: beads = finished, soaks = aniline (pro from minute one). Press white cloth on coffee. Lift, fresh part, repeat. No rubbing. Cold matters if the coffee was hot — cold prevents tannin + milk protein setting.
Dawn in cold water, wring cloth nearly dry. Wipe outside-in. Plain-water damp wipe to clear soap.
Pro-only. Reducing bleach lifts oxidized tannin from leather without stripping. Home cannot replicate. NEVER use peroxide as substitute.

steps: 1. Water-drop leather test. Aniline = pro from minute one.
3. Mild soap on barely-damp cloth, outside-in.
4. Plain-water damp wipe to clear soap.
5. Sodium hydrosulfite for residual tannin (REDUCING bleach, NOT oxidizer).
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold for blood/coffee initial blot; ambient for conditioner — no heat
warnings: NEVER hydrogen peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine — strip leather permanently
NEVER hot water — sets milk protein (with cream) + dries leather

homeSolutions.agent: Identify Your Leather First — One Drop of Water
homeSolutions.instruction: Water-drop test on hidden leather: beads = finished, soaks in = aniline (STOP, pro from minute one). Aniline absorbs coffee directly into the dyed hide; even fresh attempts may stain permanently.

The internet's worst leather-coffee advice: hydrogen peroxide, OxiClean, chlorine bleach, vinegar straight on the finish (strips the topcoat over time), baking soda paste (alkaline + abrasive). The cotton coffee-removal toolkit (peroxide) destroys leather permanently.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Damp-Cloth Blot — Press, Don't Rub
homeSolutions.instruction: Press a clean white cloth (dampened with COLD water) straight down on the coffee. Lift straight up, press fresh cloth, repeat. No rubbing — pushes coffee past the topcoat into the dyed hide. Cold matters even if the coffee was hot — cold blocks tannin and milk protein from setting.

homeSolutions.agent: Mild Soap on a BARELY-Damp Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: 1 to 2 drops of Dawn in a cup of cold water. Dampen a clean white cloth, wring IT OUT until barely damp. Wipe outside-in. Plain-water-damp wipe to clear soap. Leather should never look visibly wet.
Apply leather conditioner (Lexol or Bickmore) per the label. Cleaning strips natural oils.

homeSolutions.agent: Pro for Anything Remaining — What to Tell Them
homeSolutions.instruction: Take to a leather care specialist. Tell them: "Coffee on [finished / aniline / unknown] leather, [black / with milk / cream], age [hours / days], one home pass with cold damp cloth + mild soap + conditioner. No oxidizer, no peroxide, no acetone used."

steps: 1. Water-drop test. Aniline = pro from minute one.
2. NO peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine — DESTROYS leather.
3. NO acetone / alcohol / vinegar at home.
5. Mild soap (1–2 drops Dawn) on a BARELY-damp cloth, outside-in.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if leather is aniline, if coffee remains after one careful pass, or if anyone has applied harsh chemistry previously.
warnings: NEVER hydrogen peroxide on leather — strips permanently (the cotton trick)
NEVER straight vinegar (strips finish over time)
NEVER hot water — sets milk protein + dries leather

neverDo: Never any oxidizer (peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine) on leather
Never straight vinegar on leather finish
Never hot water — sets milk protein + dries leather
fiberSensitivities: Finished / pigmented leather: tolerates mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner

materialWarnings: Peroxide is the worst piece of bad advice for coffee on leather
Conditioner after every wet treatment, mandatory
Cold blot — even if coffee was hot

escalation.when: Aniline / unfinished leather (always), coffee remaining after one home pass, or anyone has applied harsh chemistry previously.
escalation.whatToTell: "Coffee on [finished / aniline / unknown] leather, [black / with milk / cream], age [hours / days], one home pass with cold damp cloth + mild soap + conditioner. No oxidizer, no acetone, no alcohol used."
escalation.specialistType: leather care specialist (NOT a regular dry cleaner — confirm sodium hydrosulfite + leather-safe chemistry)

contextQuestions: Water-drop test: bead, soak, or in-between?
Black coffee or coffee with milk/cream?
Has anyone applied peroxide / OxiClean / acetone?
How long ago — fresh or aged?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Coffee with cream adds protein — cold blot is even more important. Aged oxidized coffee stain on leather drifts darker and is nearly permanent without re-dyeing. Aniline leather is the unforgiving variant.
